data.title: FIFA World Cup prize money: What each USMNT player stands to earn
data.description:
By Weston Blasi

Thanks to a landmark 2022 agreement, U.S. women's soccer players will get part of the World Cup prize pool, too. Here's how it works.

An artist adds final touches to a painting featuring Argentina's Lionel Messi and Portugal's Cristiano Ronaldo.

The United States men's national soccer team plays its 2026 FIFA World Cup opener tonight - one of 48 teams competing for the famed gold trophy and a record $50 million championship prize.

However, if you're wondering how much each World Cup team gets paid to play, or what your favorite USMNT player stands to make, it can get a little complicated. The Americans operate under a unique pay model, for example.

Here's what you need to know if you want to be able to talk about World Cup compensation like a pro while watching tonight's USMNT game versus Paraguay.

For starters, FIFA doesn't actually pay any players directly. It distributes prize funds to each national team's soccer federations (like U.S. Soccer, the English Football Association or the French Football Federation); for this summer's tournament, those funds will be a record $871 million. Each nation's federation gets $12.5 million for participation in and preparation for the tournament, and collects additional funds depending on how far the team advances in the tournament.

Below are the performance-based allocated prize amounts for the 2026 FIFA World Cup: 

 
Stage of elimination             Performance prize money (USD)  Total FIFA payout (with $2.5M prep fee included) 
Group-stage exit (16 teams)      $10,000,000                    $12,500,000 
Round of 32 exit (16 teams)      $11,000,000                    $13,500,000 
Round of 16 exit (8 teams)       $15,000,000                    $17,500,000 
Quarterfinal exit (4 teams)      $19,000,000                    $21,500,000 
Fourth-place finish (1 team)     $27,000,000                    $29,500,000 
Third-place finish (1 team)      $29,000,000                    $31,500,000 
Runners-up (2nd place) (1 team)  $33,000,000                    $35,500,000 
Tournament champions (1 team)    $50,000,000                    $52,500,000

Out of the total $871 million prize-money pool, $655 million is performance-based, with the remaining amounts given for tournament participation. That breaks down to each nation being guaranteed $10 million for qualifying for the tournament, plus an additional $2.5 million for preparation grants, according to FIFA.

The prize pool is nearly doubled from the $440 million distribution for the 2022 World Cup in Qatar, mainly attributed to the growth of the game and the tournament's expansion from 32 teams to 48 teams this year.

FIFA's prize money goes directly to national football federations, but each federation has the discretion to divvy up the funds however it wants, according to FIFA's official governing documents. A federation can give equal payouts to players, coaches and team personnel, or give a bulk to players - it's up to each federation.

This makes it difficult to calculate how much each World Cup player is getting paid during the tournament, as the exact disbursement breakdown for all 48 soccer federations is not fully known.

It's been reported that the French Football Federation has offered appearance fees to players for each match played in the past. Other nations have historically given bonuses linked to tournament progress.

For example, in the 2018 tournament, 19-year-old French attacker Kylian Mbappe made roughly $500,000 after winning the tournament ($22,300 each game, plus bonuses for winning), and later announced he'd donate it to charity. Mbappe, now a Real Madrid player, has since become one of the highest-paid players in world soccer, making over $400 million throughout his career.

England's players reportedly received around GBP2,000 ($2,683) per match during the 2022 World Cup and 2024 European Championships. But for English players, it's become a longstanding tradition to donate that money to charity. Through its football federation, English players can earn additional bonuses based on team performance, although those bonus figures are not fully known.

How does that compare to possible payouts for the U.S. men's national team? The U.S. operates under a different model.

Following a landmark agreement in 2022, U.S. Soccer announced it would pool and share 80% of FIFA World Cup prize money earned by its men's and women's national teams, which created one of the most notable equal-pay arrangements in sports history. After taking its 20% cut, the federation will split the rest equally among 52 players - 26 players on the roster of each team.

"Been a long process for all sides but proud of everyone involved for reaching and achieving this. It now truly is One Nation, One Team," Tim Ream, a current USMNT defender, said about the equal-pay news back in 2022.

This year, advancing to the 2026 World Cup knockout stage could net each USMNT player around $200,000, assuming the money is split evenly among the players. Coaches, team personnel and medical personnel are paid from a separate fund, so the 80% allocation to players remains unchanged independent of those costs.

But if the USMNT were to win the World Cup - for the first time in history - its prize money would grow substantially, leading to an approximately $800,000 payout per player.

So do the teams that get paid more win more? For a few top teams, there actually appears to be an inverse relationship between the most successful teams and how much their players are paid. England and France are the two most wagered-on favorites to win the tournament, but both of those elite soccer federations reportedly pay their players a lot less than the U.S.

Ultimately, for the top soccer players in the world, the payouts for the World Cup matter less than the prestige that comes with actually playing for your country and winning in the tournament. Part of that may be because international soccer, like the World Cup, is their side hustle, while their "day jobs" playing for their club teams are where they earn their real salary. Some of the highest-paid players at the World Cup include Portugal's Cristiano Ronaldo (Saudi Arabia's Al-Nassr, $300 million), Argentina's Lionel Messi (MLS's Inter Miami, $140 million), France's Kylian Mbappé (Spain's Real Madrid, $95 million) and Norway's Erling Haaland (England's Manchester City, $80 million), according to Forbes.

The 2026 World Cup, which is being co-hosted by the U.S., Canada and Mexico, is underway after Mexico triumphed over South Africa 2-0 on Thursday. The USMNT squares off against Paraguay on Friday at 9 p.m. Eastern time.

Read: We asked AI to predict the 2026 World Cup winner. It picked a country that's never won.

-Weston Blasi

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  06-12-26 1744ET

data.title: Marathon Petroleum Named to Dow Jones Best-in-Class Indices
data.description:
Key Points

  • Marathon Petroleum was named to the Dow Jones Best‑in‑Class Indices based on its performance in the annual S&P Global Corporate Sustainability Assessment.

  • The company earned a top‑quartile ESG score within the North American Oil and Gas Refining and Marketing sector and scored above the industry average across governance & economic, environmental, and social criteria.

  • The recognition reflects Marathon Petroleum's continued focus on sustainability, operational excellence and continuous improvement in a changing energy industry.

NORTHAMPTON, MA / ACCESS Newswire / June 12, 2026 / Marathon Petroleum was named to the annual Dow Jones Best-in-Class Indices as a result of its performance on the annual S&P Global Corporate Sustainability Assessment (CSA).

Marathon Petroleum earned a top quartile CSA score for the North American Oil and Gas Refining and Marketing sector and scored above industry average in all three score dimensions ─ governance & economics, environmental, and social.

"This recognition reflects Marathon Petroleum's continued focus on sustainability and operational excellence," said Andrea Salimbene, Director of ESG Strategy at Marathon Petroleum. "Our sustainability-driven approach supports our relentless commitment to continuously improve as we contribute to an evolving energy industry."

S&P Dow Jones Indices is the largest global resource for essential index-based concepts, data and research, and home to iconic financial market indicators, such as the S&P 500® and the Dow Jones Industrial Average®. The S&P Dow Jones Indices select companies for inclusion using an industry‑relative sustainability evaluation.


Lima Pipeline Terminal in Ohio

Find more stories and multimedia from Marathon Petroleum at 3blmedia.com.

Contact Info:

Website: https://www.3blmedia.com/profiles/marathon-petroleum-corporation

data.title: Tech Up, Paring Weekly Losses - Tech Roundup
data.description:
Shares of technology companies rose, further paring losses for a volatile week, after an auspicious market debut for SpaceX. Shares of the rocketry and artificial-intelligence company rose by roughly 19% to $161.11.

One strategist said some investors were already placing bearish bets on SpaceX, a dangerous strategy.

"On paper, SpaceX is a short seller's dream setup: massive valuation, complex corporate structure, and a founder who splits his attention across five different companies and is always late on hitting ambitious growth targets," said technology strategist Luke Lango, in a note to clients. "But 'on paper' is doing a lot of heavy lifting. This is a company that controls over half of all global orbital launch capacity, is cash-flow positive, and has Starlink as a genuine recurring revenue machine with 8.5 million subscribers and growing."

The PHLX SOX Semiconductor continued to storm back from last week's historic rout. One hedge-fund manager said the parabolic gains of chip and AI stocks was reminiscent of speculative bubbles, but warned they could continue to behave that way for some time.

"It smells like a bubble and behaves like one, but bubbles can last longer than expected," said Lorenzo Di Mattia, manager of hedge fund Sibilla Global Fund, in a note to clients. 




  Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com 



  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  June 12, 2026 17:41 ET (21:41 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2026 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: I'm 55 and earn $100,000. Should I take a $2,900 monthly pension - or $2,200 with 3% annual hikes?
data.description:
By Quentin Fottrell

'I plan to continue working until age 60'

"I have no other retirement accounts, so my retirement income will consist primarily of this pension and Social Security." (Photo subject is a model.)

Dear Quentin,

Can you help me decide which pension option to take? I have the choice of receiving either a pension of $2,900 per month, or $2,200 per month with an annual 3% increase. For background, I am 55 and I plan to continue working until age 60.

This pension will not be my sole source of income for the next few years. I earn $100,000 a year. I have no other retirement accounts, so my retirement income will consist of this pension and Social Security. I appreciate any insight you can offer.

Working Until 60

Related: Our financial adviser keeps pushing annuities even after we said no. Should we fire him?

You can email The Moneyist with any financial and ethical questions at qfottrell@marketwatch.com. The Moneyist regrets he cannot reply to questions individually.

What if you invested that $700 every month for the next 19 years, and spent the rest living your best life?

Dear Working,

Life is rarely simple, but retirement is nearly always complicated.

It's an interesting dilemma. The flat pension, which does not keep up with inflation increases, is substantially higher. It would take a long time to catch up with the lower pension with those 3% annual increases - 19 years, in fact.

Those 19 years are a long time because as you age you may experience health setbacks and reduced mobility, among other issues. You may even die before those nearly two decades elapse, so you would have lost out on all that extra loot.

For that reason, I suggest you take the $2,900-a-month pension. There are actually two points at which you break even. With the 3% annual increase, the lower pension finally exceeds the pension with the extra $700 per month after nine years.

The second breakeven point - the big one - comes at 19 years; because your $2,900 pension provides a significantly larger payment from the beginning, it takes nearly two decades to exceed the cumulative sum you earn over that time.

It takes 19 years to cumulatively exceed the amount your pension gives you.

Another incentive to take the higher sum: What if you invested that $700 every month for the next 19 years, and spent the rest living your best life? Assuming a 7% average annual return, compounded monthly, you would have roughly $330,000.

So by that all-important 19th year, the cumulative amount received from the two pension options would be equal. If you had invested $700 each month and earned an average 7% annual return, you could also have built a six-figure nest egg at 74.

This is a variation on the Social Security dilemma, which is more about waiting for a higher amount than taking a lower amount now. You have the option of taking either the lower or higher figure (with annual increases) at age 55.

You will face that question when you near retirement: Do you take your Social Security at 62, 67 (your full retirement age) or wait until you are 70 so you can avail yourself of those 8% annual bumps after your 67th birthday?

Don't miss: My elderly mother and I own a home together. Will Medicaid force its sale?

A couple of warnings

But back to your pension dilemma: A 7% market return is an assumption based on historical averages - it is not a guarantee. The 3% annual increase, assuming the pension fund is on solid ground, is legally guaranteed.

Retiring at 60 is a noble goal, but be mindful that your Medicare does not kick in until you are 65, so you may end up spending a chunk of your available income on private medical insurance. That could be a costly five years.

Health insurance would likely become your biggest new monthly expense if you decide to retire before age 65, so be sure to factor those costs into your budget alongside other major obligations, such as a mortgage/rent, utilities, credit-card debt and car payments.

Monthly premiums of $1,000 or more can quickly eat into your retirement savings, particularly during those crucial early years of retirement. In addition to premiums, expect to pay deductibles, copayments, coinsurance and prescription-drug costs.

Retiring at 60 is noble, but your Medicare does not kick in until you are 65.

You have two considerations, the answers to which will tell you everything you need to know: 1) Do you want to chase higher growth through the market with a higher fixed amount, or accept a guaranteed income that aims to keep up with inflation?

And 2) how long do you expect to live? If you see yourself living well beyond your 70s and into your 80s and 90s, you may prefer the lower pension with the annual 3% increase. It's a trade-off between longevity and risk tolerance.

One final thought: You don't have other retirement accounts, so the option with the 3% annual increase functions as a form of longevity insurance. If you do live into your 80s or 90s - here's hoping - those 3% hikes will help preserve your purchasing power.

The higher pension without the annual increase, on the other hand, places more responsibility on you to invest wisely and perhaps avoid spending the extra income. Given that you will get Social Security, I lean towards this option.

That hypothetical $330,000 nest egg, while not guaranteed, is hard to resist.

Related: 'I feel like I'm living a lie': My husband and I pretend we're strapped for cash in front of friends. Is that bad?

The Moneyist regrets he cannot respond to letters individually. Check out The Moneyist's private Facebook group, where members help answer life's thorniest money issues. Post your questions, or weigh in on the latest Moneyist columns.

More columns from Quentin Fottrell:

'It will not bring you happiness': I have advice for your single, childless 62-year-old multimillionaire reader

'This would be a one-time event': How can I take extra money from my 401(k) without triggering higher Medicare premiums?

My husband won't sell his home so we can live together full time. Is this a red flag after a decade of marriage?

By emailing your questions to The Moneyist or posting your dilemmas on The Moneyist Facebook group, you agree to have them published anonymously on MarketWatch.

By submitting your story to Dow Jones & Co., the publisher of MarketWatch, you understand and agree that we may use your story, or versions of it, in all media and platforms, including via third parties.

-Quentin Fottrell

data.title: KBRA Assigns Preliminary Ratings to OBX 2026-AHC2 Trust
data.description:
KBRA Assigns Preliminary Ratings to OBX 2026-AHC2 Trust

KBRA assigns preliminary ratings to 65 classes of mortgage pass-through notes from OBX 2026-AHC2 Trust, a prime agency-eligible RMBS transaction sponsored by Onslow Bay Financial LLC, that is fully originated and serviced by AmeriHome Mortgage Company, LLC (AmeriHome). This transaction is comprised of 599 residential mortgages with an aggregate unpaid principal balance (UPB) of approximately $340.0 million as of the June 1, 2026 cut-off date. The underlying collateral consists primarily of 30-year fixed-rate qualified mortgages (FRMs).

KBRA’s rating approach incorporated loan-level analysis of the mortgage pool through its Residential Asset Loss Model (REALM), an examination of the results from third-party loan file due diligence, cash flow modeling analysis of the transaction’s payment structure, reviews of key transaction parties and an assessment of the transaction’s legal structure and documentation. This analysis is further described in our U.S. RMBS Rating Methodology.

To access ratings and relevant documents, click here.

Click here to view the report.

Recent Publications

  • RMBS KCAT
  • OBX 2026-AHC2 Trust Tear Sheet

Methodologies

  • RMBS: U.S. RMBS Rating Methodology
  • Structured Finance: Global Structured Finance Counterparty Methodology

Disclosures

Further information on key credit considerations, sensitivity analyses that consider what factors can affect these credit ratings and how they could lead to an upgrade or a downgrade, and ESG factors (where they are a key driver behind the change to the credit rating or rating outlook) can be found in the full rating report referenced above.

A description of all substantially material sources that were used to prepare the credit rating and information on the methodology(ies) (inclusive of any material models and sensitivity analyses of the relevant key rating assumptions, as applicable) used in determining the credit rating is available in the Information Disclosure Form(s) located here.

Information on the meaning of each rating category can be located here.

Further disclosures relating to this rating action are available in the Information Disclosure Form(s) referenced above. Additional information regarding KBRA policies, methodologies, rating scales and disclosures are available at www.kbra.com.

About KBRA

Kroll Bond Rating Agency, LLC (KBRA), one of the major credit rating agencies (CRA), is a full-service CRA registered with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission as an NRSRO. Kroll Bond Rating Agency Europe Limited is registered as a CRA with the European Securities and Markets Authority. Kroll Bond Rating Agency UK Limited is registered as a CRA with the UK Financial Conduct Authority. In addition, KBRA is designated as a Designated Rating Organization (DRO) by the Ontario Securities Commission for issuers of asset-backed securities to file a short form prospectus or shelf prospectus. KBRA is also recognized as a Qualified Rating Agency by Taiwan’s Financial Supervisory Commission and is recognized by the National Association of Insurance Commissioners as a Credit Rating Provider (CRP) in the U.S.

data.title: In HelloNation, Home Construction Expert Bob Pack of Howard, Ohio Explains Lake Home Building Challenges in Central Ohio
data.description:
In HelloNation, Home Construction Expert Bob Pack of Howard, Ohio Explains Lake Home Building Challenges in Central Ohio

In HelloNation, Home Construction Expert Bob Pack of Howard, Ohio Explains Lake Home Building Challenges in Central Ohio

PR Newswire

HOWARD, Ohio, June 12, 2026

HOWARD, Ohio, June 12, 2026 /PRNewswire/ -- How does building a lake home differ from standard home construction? A HelloNation article outlines the key differences, offering a practical look at the unique planning, budgeting, and construction decisions required for lakefront living in central Ohio near Apple Valley Lake. The article highlights important steps homeowners should take before breaking ground to ensure both compliance and long-term durability.

The article begins by pointing out that lake home construction in central Ohio involves more than just proximity to the water. Regulations surrounding shoreline setbacks, soil conditions, and environmental protections can influence where and how the home is built. Reviewing these rules early is critical. The article notes that failing to do so can result in costly adjustments to layout and placement if the project is already underway.

While moisture control is important for any home in central Ohio, one of the more unique challenges of lakefront construction is the steep slopes often associated with shoreline lots. Proper grading and drainage design become critical in these situations. If runoff and slope stability are not handled correctly, homeowners can face erosion issues, water management problems, and long-term structural concerns. The article explains that designing effective runoff systems and planning for slope conditions can protect both the home and the surrounding land while meeting environmental regulations.

Planning for recreational use is another area that makes lake home construction unique. The article emphasizes the importance of early decisions about features like a boat dock or sea wall. These elements not only add to the enjoyment of the property but also come with their own set of zoning requirements and budget implications. Understanding how these features interact with local regulations helps avoid delays and ensures the final layout works for the homeowner's intended use.

Timing is also a major factor when building a lake home in central Ohio. The article explains that some lakes lower their water levels at certain times of the year to allow shoreline improvements, including dock construction and erosion control. Homeowners who plan construction during these periods can complete critical stages more efficiently. This seasonal coordination requires input from a builder familiar with local lake systems.

Choosing the right contractor is essential. The HelloNation article recommends selecting a builder with experience in lake home and waterfront construction. These projects demand a strong understanding of shoreline regulations, soil types, foundation requirements specific to waterfront lots, and the additional grading and retaining wall structures often required for sloped sites. The article advises open and continuous communication between the builder and homeowner to ensure the design meets lifestyle expectations while maintaining structural integrity.

Design features play a role in both enjoyment and practicality. While many lake homes include large windows or decks to maximize views, the article stresses that these elements must be balanced with foundation stability and environmental concerns. It also suggests using water-resistant materials and energy-efficient systems to address temperature and humidity fluctuations common near water. These decisions impact both comfort and long-term maintenance.

The HelloNation article makes clear that lakefront building requires careful planning, from shoreline setbacks to recreational use and environmental compliance. Factoring in moisture control, durable materials, and seasonal timing gives homeowners the best chance for a successful project. By collaborating with experienced professionals and setting realistic expectations, homeowners can enjoy the benefits of a well-built lake home in central Ohio.

Key Differences in Lake Home Construction in Central Ohio features insights from Bob Pack, Home Construction Expert of Howard, Ohio, in HelloNation.

About HelloNation
HelloNation is a premier media platform that connects readers with trusted professionals and businesses across various industries. Through its innovative "edvertising" approach that blends educational content and storytelling, HelloNation delivers expert-driven articles that inform, inspire, and empower. Covering topics from home improvement and health to business strategy and lifestyle, HelloNation highlights leaders making a meaningful impact in their communities.

SOURCE HelloNation

data.title: How Insurance Expert Dennis Chaumont of Edmond, Oklahoma Breaks Down Renters Insurance for HelloNation
data.description:
How Insurance Expert Dennis Chaumont of Edmond, Oklahoma Breaks Down Renters Insurance for HelloNation

How Insurance Expert Dennis Chaumont of Edmond, Oklahoma Breaks Down Renters Insurance for HelloNation

PR Newswire

EDMOND, Okla., June 12, 2026

EDMOND, Okla., June 12, 2026 /PRNewswire/ -- How much renters insurance does someone really need for an apartment? That's the focus of a HelloNation article that offers a clear explanation of coverage types, common exclusions, and key decisions renters must make. With insights from Insurance Expert Dennis Chaumont of Edmond, Oklahoma, the article outlines what this policy covers—and what it doesn't—for those living in leased apartments or rental homes.

The article explains that renters insurance is often misunderstood or overlooked, especially by first-time tenants in Edmond, Oklahoma. Many renters mistakenly believe their landlord's insurance covers their personal belongings. The HelloNation piece makes it clear that renters insurance is designed to fill this gap, offering financial protection for personal property, liability, and living expenses after a covered event.

Personal property coverage is a main feature of renters insurance. According to the article, this includes everyday items such as electronics, furniture, clothing, and kitchenware. If these belongings are damaged or lost due to fire, theft, or another covered incident, renters insurance may help replace them—up to the policy's coverage limits. The article advises renters to create a simple inventory to better estimate the actual value of their belongings and select the right coverage limits accordingly.

Theft coverage receives special attention in the article. It notes that renters insurance doesn't just cover items stolen from inside the apartment. In many cases, personal property is also protected if stolen from a car, hotel room, or other temporary location. These protections can be helpful for renters in Edmond who travel or keep valuables in their vehicles. However, the article also cautions that exclusions and limitations apply, especially with high-value items.

Liability coverage is another key benefit explained in the article. If a guest is injured inside the rented unit or if the renter causes damage to another person's property, liability coverage may help pay for medical costs or legal fees. As Insurance Expert Dennis Chaumont explains through the article, many renters choose higher liability coverage because the added premium is typically small compared to the potential costs of an accident.

The article also highlights additional living expenses coverage, which may help with temporary housing and meals if the apartment becomes unlivable due to a covered loss. In Edmond, where extreme weather or internal water damage can force a sudden move-out, this portion of renters insurance can reduce financial stress.

Just like homeowners insurance, renters policies do not cover flood damage. The article clarifies that any damage caused by rising water, heavy rain, or surface runoff requires a separate flood insurance policy. This is an important insurance exclusion for Edmond residents, where weather events can bring flooding risks to even second-story apartments.

Other limits and exclusions are also addressed. Renters insurance does not cover belongings owned by roommates unless all names are on the same policy. Damage from illegal activity or intentional actions is also excluded. The article stresses the importance of understanding these boundaries to avoid surprises when filing a claim.

By walking readers through the basics of renters insurance, the article gives Edmond renters the tools they need to make informed choices. Knowing how coverage limits, theft coverage, and liability coverage work helps reduce uncertainty and improves peace of mind.

How Much Renters Insurance Do You Need for an Apartment? features insights from Dennis Chaumont, Insurance Expert of Edmond, Oklahoma, in HelloNation.

About HelloNation
HelloNation is a premier media platform that connects readers with trusted professionals and businesses across various industries.
